---
title: "PromptLab: AI Prompt Engineering Studio"
author: "PromptLab Team"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# PromptLab: AI Prompt Engineering Studio

## Project Summary

PromptLab is a web-based toolkit designed to help users diagnose, score, optimize, and manage prompts for large language models. The project addresses a common problem in everyday AI use: many people know how to ask a question, but they do not know how to formulate a clear, structured, and high-quality prompt. This is especially relevant for students, creators, and professionals who want faster and more reliable results from AI tools. Practical prompt engineering guidance emphasizes the importance of role definition, context, constraints, and output format in producing better responses [@openai_prompt_engineering; @anthropic_prompt_engineering].

## Problem Statement

Users often receive unsatisfactory or inconsistent outputs because their prompts are too vague, missing critical context, or lacking clear instructions. PromptLab addresses this need by offering a lightweight workflow that helps users improve prompts before sending them to an AI system. The system focuses on transparency and usability, showing both the diagnosis and the reason behind each improvement.

## System Overview

The technical core of PromptLab includes three main modules: Prompt Lint, Prompt Score, and Prompt Optimizer. Prompt Lint checks whether the user prompt contains key elements such as task, role, context, output format, constraints, examples, and clarity. Prompt Score evaluates the prompt across multiple dimensions, including clarity, role setting, task clarity, context, constraints, output format, and executability. Each dimension receives a score, and the final score is calculated as the average of all dimension scores. Prompt Optimizer then improves the original prompt based on the diagnosis by adding missing components such as a clearer role, a more structured task, a stronger output format, and practical requirements.

## Evaluation and Results

The project was evaluated through a small survey and a set of representative test prompts. The survey results suggest that many participants frequently use AI tools but still struggle to write prompts that produce consistent and useful outputs. The test cases showed that the optimized prompts were more complete and easier to follow than the original versions, which supports the value of structured prompt analysis and guided improvement.

## Teamwork and Collaboration

The project was completed through a clear division of labor among four members. Member A focused on the backend logic for prompt analysis, scoring, and optimization, while Member B built the Streamlit interface and prepared the live demo experience. Member C collected survey data, designed test prompts, and produced the evaluation figures and findings. Member D was responsible for product storytelling, documentation, the final report, marketing material, and the overall integration of deliverables. The team used Git for version control and followed a branch-based workflow with task-oriented commits. Representative history entries include commits such as feat: add prompt scoring function, feat: implement prompt optimizer, feat: create streamlit homepage, data: add survey results, and docs: add final report draft, which reflect the distributed contribution model and the collaboration process described in the project documentation. The repository history shows how each contribution was merged into the final project, demonstrating a realistic team workflow rather than a single-person effort.

## Use of AI Tools

AI tools were used at several stages of the project for brainstorming, drafting documentation, refining the project narrative, and checking the clarity of explanations. The team explicitly used GitHub Copilot for code suggestions and drafting support, and ChatGPT for report structuring, wording refinement, and debugging ideas. These tools helped accelerate development and documentation, but the final content was reviewed by the team for accuracy, quality, and originality. The report therefore reflects a human-verified workflow rather than an unattended AI-only submission.

## Limitations

The current version of PromptLab is a lightweight rule-based prototype rather than a production system connected to a real LLM API. The diagnosis and scoring logic are based on heuristic checks and predefined rules, and the optimization suggestions are generated through structured templates and rule-based transformations rather than by calling OpenAI, DeepSeek, or Gemini directly. This means the system is explainable and easy to test, but it may be less flexible than a fully model-driven product. In addition, the current evaluation is based on a small sample of survey responses and test prompts, which limits the strength of the conclusions. Future work should focus on integrating real model APIs, expanding the evaluation dataset, and improving the persistence and collaboration features of the platform.

## Conclusion

PromptLab demonstrates a practical and accessible approach to helping users improve their prompts with a simple, explainable workflow. The project combines prompt analysis, scoring, optimization, and documentation in one coherent experience, and it provides a strong foundation for future development toward a more powerful and fully integrated AI-assisted prompt engineering tool.
